content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

January 3, 2017

Jeffrey G. Scott
Law Offices Scott & Jackson
16935 West Bernardo Drive, Suite 170
San Diego, CA 92127

Re:     Your Request for Advice
        Our File No. A-16-246

Dear Mr. Scott:

        This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest. Moreover, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTIONS

1. May Betty Evans, a member of the Vallecitos Water District (the "Water District"), take part in decisions regarding the Water District's Master Plan where she owns real property located approximately 2,500 feet from a proposed development?

2. May Director Evans take part in decisions to approve a Water Supply Assessment, Water Supply Verification and an agreement with the developer relating to the proposed development?

CONCLUSIONS

1. Yes.

2. No. It is reasonably foreseeable that these decisions will have a material financial effect on Director Evans' real property.

FACTS

_________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-16-246
Page No. 2

The Vallecitos Water District (the "Water District") is a county water district in San Diego County and is organized and existing under California Water Code Section 30000, et seq. As a water supplier to nearly 90,000 people in its service area, the Water District is required to prepare and update its Master Plan (the "Master Plan") and Urban Water Management Plan (the "Management Plan") every five years. The Master Plan and Management Plan help to ensure that the Water District has adequate infrastructure and water supplies to meet existing and future water demands including future demands created by land-use decisions made by cities and counties in its service area.

The update of the Management Plan has been recently completed and approved.

The Water District is now working on the update of the Master Plan which is anticipated to come before the Water District Board in Spring 2017. The Master Plan pertains to the Water District's entire water and wastewater system. Future water demand is based on current land uses, approved land uses and land uses that are projected by growth-based computer modeling. Thus, the Master Plan does not take into account any potential development projects that have not yet been approved by the city or county, as applicable. The Water District has no power to regulate or control land use.

Newland Sierra LLC (the "Developer") is proposing a major subdivision development (the "Project") of approximately 1,985 acres in San Diego County that would permit existing agricultural acreage to become a 2,135 home development, 81,000 square feet of general commercial space set aside for shopping and small retail, a school site, 37 acres of park space and 1,202 acres of biological open space.

The Water District is responsible for preparing a Water Supply Assessment to be included in the Project's environmental documentation and issuing an affirmative written Water Supply Verification that verifies sufficient water supplies are available for the Project prior to approval of a tentative map.

The Water District must also enter into an agreement with the Developer called "Agreement for Construction of Facilities to be Dedicated to the Vallecitos Water District" (the "Facilities Agreement"). The purpose of the Facilities Agreement is to ensure that the Developer constructs improvements, pays its fair share of fees and complies with all of the District's rules applicable to the Project. The District agrees to provide water services to the Project if the Developer agrees to construct capital improvements that may include main lines, reservoirs, pump stations and trunk lines. The plans for these improvements must be reviewed and approved by the District. The Developer must also post payment, performance and warranty bonds, pay all fees and charges, grant easements and, in some cases, dedicate real property.

The County cannot approve the Project until the Water District Board has approved the Water Supply Assessment, Water Supply Verification and Facilities Agreement.

A member of the Water District Board, Betty Evans, owns and resides on property located less than 2,500 feet from the boundaries of the Project.

File No. A-16-246
Page No. 3

You state that the Project is likely to impact Director Evans' real property, both in a positive
and negative way. On the negative side, during construction, property in the vicinity of Director
Evans' property would be impacted by significantly increased noise and traffic and lower air
quality. If the Project is built out, there would likely be permanent increases in vehicle traffic along
Twin Oaks Valley/Deer Spring Road which are access roads to Director Evans' property. On the
positive side, the Project may increase property values as it is likely to come with significant road
and traffic management improvements and more convenient access to shopping, schools, and parks.

ANALYSIS

Under Section 87100, the general rule is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public
official makes, participates in making, or uses his or her position to influence a governmental
decision which has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of the
official's financial interests.

You have identified one interest, Director Evans' real property, that may result in a conflict
of interest.

Foreseeability

A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. The standard for foreseeability
differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in the decision. Under the facts you
have provided, Director Evans' property is not explicitly involved in decisions involving the Master
Plan, Water Supply Assessment, Water Facilities Agreement. Thus, as applied to her property, Regulation 18701(b) provides: "In general, if the financial effect can be
recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably
foreseeable. If the financial result cannot be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not
subject to the public official's control, it is not reasonably foreseeable."

Materiality

As relevant to the facts here, the financial effect of the Water District Board's decisions
regarding the Master Plan on Director Evans' real property is material if the decisions:

• Would change the character of her real property by substantially altering traffic levels or
intensity of use, including parking, of property surrounding her parcel, the view, privacy,
noise levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the
market value of her real property. (Regulation 18702.2(a)(10);

• Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and consideration under the
circumstances, to believe that the decisions are of such a nature that its reasonably
foreseeable effects would influence the market value of the official's property (Regulation
18702.2(a) 12).

File No. A-16-246
Page No. 4

Master Plan

The Master Plan is a tool for planning for growth within the District by evaluating existing
and future needs for water, wastewater and recycled water services, and developing a facilities plan
and capital improvement program to accommodate these needs. The Master Plan pertains to the
Water District's entire water and wastewater systems. The Master Plan does not (and cannot under
CEQA) take into account developments that have not been approved by the county. Thus, because
the County has not yet approved the Project, the Master Plan is not based on any anticipated water
needs that may result from the Project. The Water District has no power to control or regulate land
use. As a mere planning tool, the Master Plan does not bind the County to any actions or drive the
County's decisions to approve projects. In approving a project, the County does not rely on the
Master Plan, but, rather, upon the Water District's evaluation of each individual project. Under
these facts, we do not believe that it is reasonably foreseeable that decisions regarding the update of
the Master Plan will have a material financial effect on Director Evans' real property.

Water Supply Assessment, Water Supply Verification and Facilities Agreement

Unlike the Master Plan, the Water Supply Assessment and Water Supply Verification are
specific to the Project and will be relied upon by the County in approving the Project. Also, the
Facilities Agreement between the Water District and the Project's developer must be in place before
the County can approve the Project.

You state that during the Project's construction, Director Evans' property would be
impacted by significantly increased noise and traffic and lower air quality. We do not believe that
construction-related impacts, which would only be temporary, would likely have a material effect
on the value of Director Evans' property.

However, in addressing permanent impacts, we note that Director Evans' property is located
in a sparsely populated area on a 2.5-acre lot and is located less than one-half mile from the Project
area. Under the proposed zoning changes, the 2,300-acre Project site which is currently zoned for
agricultural use and a density of approximately 100 homes would permit 2,100 homes on the site.
This is twenty-one times more homes than is currently permitted. You state that if the Project is
built out, there would likely be significant increases in noise and traffic, particularly on the
community's access roads, and lower air quality.

Notwithstanding the potential benefits you have described (improved roads, traffic
management and conveniently located shopping, schools and parks), we believe that the major
increase in density, with corresponding increases in traffic and noise, would likely have a material
effect on the market value of Director Evans' property. Under these facts, we find that decisions
regarding the Water Supply Assessment, Water Supply Verification and Facilities Agreement will
have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on Director Evans' property and, therefore,
she may not participate in these decisions.2

2 When a public official who holds an office specified in Section 87200 has a conflict of interest in a decision
noticed at a public meeting, then he or she must: (1) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, orally identify each
type of economic interest involved in the decision as well as details of the economic interest on the record of the
meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself, and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item.
(Section 87105; Regulation 18707.)

File No. A-16-246
Page No. 5

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: Valentina Joyce
Counsel, Legal Division

VJ:jgl

2. May Director Evans take part in decisions to approve a Water Supply Assessment, 
Water Supply Verification and an agreement with the developer relating to the proposed 
development?

l. Yes. 
2. No. It is reasonably foreseeable that these decisions will have a material financial effect 
on Director Evans' real property.
